title: In the Middle East and North Africa, America and China Converge More Than They Diverge
gist: Carnegie's Amr Hamzawy and Kathryn Selfe argue U.S. and Chinese interests in the Middle East and North Africa converge more than they clash — both want stable trade flows through the region's chokepoints — and that Washington's military/security dominance and Beijing's economic/infrastructure rise are complementary rather than zero-sum, letting MENA middle powers hedge profitably between the two.

themes.theme: Shared great-power interest in MENA stability

insights.claim: The U.S. and China share a primary interest in MENA stability because both need free trade through the region's maritime chokepoints, a fact underscored by the war with Iran disrupting shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: A durable, structural reason MENA won't become a full-blown US-China proxy battleground — both powers are incentivized to keep trade routes open, capping tail risk from chokepoint conflict.
insights.locator: The United States and China are united by their primary interest in MENA: free trade.

insights.claim: Washington's core MENA interest is Israel's security while Beijing's is securing oil and LNG supply, and these two priorities are compatible rather than competing because both require regional peace and stability.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: Explains why US-China rivalry doesn't automatically translate into MENA proxy conflict — their bottom-line interests point the same direction.
insights.locator: Washington is also driven by interest in Israel’s security

themes.theme: Military asymmetry favors the U.S., by design on China's part

insights.claim: The U.S. holds an overwhelming military edge in MENA, supplying 64.3% of the region's arms imports in 2025 versus zero recorded Chinese deliveries that year, and maintains defense agreements or bases with Bahrain, Jordan, Kuwait, Qatar, the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman, and Egypt plus Major Non-NATO Ally status for nine states.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: US arms and basing dominance in MENA is not being seriously contested militarily — the competition with China is playing out in economic and diplomatic channels, not defense.
insights.locator: According to the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute, in 2025

insights.claim: China deliberately avoids formal defense commitments in MENA, instead running strategic and comprehensive partnerships with limited security cooperation, while its UN peacekeeping personnel contribution in the region is consistently more than double that of the United States.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: China is building regional goodwill cheaply through peacekeeping rather than costly basing/alliance commitments — a soft-power arbitrage the US can't easily replicate given its treaty-heavy posture.
insights.locator: Compared to a vast network of U.S. bases

insights.claim: China's light military footprint is a strategic choice that has paid reputational dividends by avoiding association with regional wars, but it also leaves Beijing unable to respond to crises — China had to mount a costly evacuation of nationals from Libya in 2011, was absent from the 2023 twenty-member naval coalition against Houthi shipping attacks, and by 2026 lacked the capacity to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz despite roughly 50% of its crude oil imports transiting it.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: China's non-military posture is a real constraint, not just optics — it cannot yet protect its own energy security interests in a MENA crisis, a structural vulnerability investors should weight against China's rising economic footprint.
insights.quote: China’s position of military inferiority is not necessarily a weakness—Beijing benefits greatly from the U.S. security regime in MENA.
insights.locator: China’s position of military inferiority is not necessarily a weakness

themes.theme: China's economic and infrastructure lead over the U.S.

insights.claim: China's trade with the Gulf Cooperation Council states reached $288 billion in 2024, more than three times the $86 billion in GCC-U.S. trade, making China the top import partner for most MENA states.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: China has already won the MENA trade relationship at scale; US economic engagement in the region is structurally behind and unlikely to close the gap without a dedicated push beyond AI infrastructure.
insights.locator: In 2024, China reported a $288 billion total trade value

insights.claim: MENA was the top recipient of China's Belt and Road Initiative investment in 2024 at $39 billion, a 102% year-on-year surge, spanning port, rail, and telecom modernization, while the U.S.'s comparable infrastructure investment has been limited and ad hoc apart from an emerging build-out of AI data centers and semiconductor partnerships in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the UAE.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: AI data-center and chip partnerships are effectively the one lane where the US is out-investing China in MENA infrastructure — a signal for where US strategic capital is concentrating in the region.
insights.locator: The biggest strength of China’s economic model is infrastructure.

insights.claim: Trump's dismantling of USAID in early 2025 eliminated 83% of the agency's contracts and cut $60 billion in aid worldwide, hitting MENA especially hard given the region's deep aid dependence, with USAID's MENA allocation having been $4.2 billion in 2024 versus $3.7 billion in 2023.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: The US aid retreat opens a vacuum that China and Gulf donors are positioned to fill, though the piece notes Gulf states may prove less willing donors than partners hope — a gap likely to widen China's soft-power lead further.
insights.locator: U.S. President Donald Trump’s dismantling of USAID

insights.claim: Arab Barometer polling shows U.S. favorability collapsed after October 7, 2023 — Tunisian favorability fell from 40% to 10% within three weeks — while China's favorability rose in the same countries; in the most recent wave, only 32.8% of respondents viewed the U.S. favorably (67.2% unfavorable) versus 64.3% favorable for China (35.8% unfavorable).
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: A stark, measurable reversal in regional public opinion that underlies China's soft-power gains — relevant to any thesis about US brand risk from its Gaza policy bleeding into broader MENA commercial and diplomatic relationships.
insights.quote: just 32.8 percent of participants viewed the United States favorably, compared to 67.2 percent who viewed it unfavorably; 64.3 percent held favorable views of China, compared to 35.8 percent unfavorable.
insights.locator: In the most recent wave of Arab Barometer data

insights.claim: The author attributes the U.S. diplomatic setback in MENA to self-inflicted causes — the Muslim travel ban, visa restrictions, complicity in Gaza's devastation, and the war with Iran — rather than to superior Chinese diplomacy, arguing this has 'handed a diplomatic victory over to China on a silver platter.'
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: Frames China's regional diplomatic gains as a byproduct of US self-sabotage rather than Chinese strategic superiority — implies the trend could reverse under a different US policy posture rather than being structurally locked in.
insights.quote: self-sabotage—in the form of the Muslim travel ban, visa restrictions, abetting the atrocities in Gaza, and now a war in Iran—has handed a diplomatic victory over to China on a silver platter.
insights.locator: Despite the United States’s greater involvement in mediation

insights.claim: China's core diplomatic asset in MENA is having no adversaries in the region — it maintains full relations with every state including Israel — whereas the U.S. lacks relations with Iran and has suspended embassy operations in Libya and Yemen.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: China's zero-adversary diplomatic posture gives it mediation optionality the US structurally lacks in the current environment, reinforcing China's role as a convening power (e.g., Iran-Saudi, Israel-Iran mediation).
insights.locator: China’s strongest asset is its lack of adversaries.

insights.claim: Despite China's polling and mediation gains, the U.S. retains a far deeper institutional footprint, with more than 600 treaties in force across MENA spanning agriculture, defense, education, and trade, versus China's narrower treaty portfolio concentrated in economic topics like bilateral investment and double-taxation agreements plus nine judicial assistance treaties.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: The US retains a structural institutional moat in MENA that China's economic and public-diplomacy gains haven't yet displaced — depth of ties still favors Washington even as favorability polling favors Beijing.
insights.locator: However, Washington has a much deeper and more diverse portfolio of treaties

themes.theme: MENA middle powers hedge for leverage

insights.claim: MENA states extract concessions from the U.S. by threatening to buy from China: Saudi Arabia turned to China for ballistic missile technology the U.S. restricts under the Missile Technology Control Regime, Egypt explored China's J-10C jet after the U.S. denied it F-35s, and Egypt, Iraq, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE bought Chinese CH-4 and Wing Loong armed drones after U.S. export rules blocked their ambitions — pressure that later caused Washington to loosen its own UAV export restrictions.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: A concrete mechanism by which MENA hedging forces US policy concessions — useful precedent for predicting how Gulf states will use Chinese alternatives as leverage in ongoing AI-chip export negotiations.
insights.locator: Today, militarily, the primary arena for hedging is in specific arms deals.

insights.claim: In technology, Gulf states led by Saudi Arabia and the UAE hedge by threatening to adopt cheaper Chinese AI alternatives, which has pushed Washington to ease export restrictions on advanced chips; in turn, the Gulf states have reduced reliance on Chinese providers like Huawei in sensitive infrastructure once U.S. access improved.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: Direct evidence of the Gulf leveraging Chinese AI/tech competition to win better US chip access terms — relevant to semiconductor export-policy and AI-infrastructure investment theses in the Gulf.
insights.locator: Technology is another emerging arena for MENA actors to hedge

insights.claim: Nearly all MENA states participate in multilateral bodies aligned with both great powers simultaneously — sixteen of nineteen MENA states are in the China-led Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank while also receiving World Bank funding, and Egypt, Iran, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE are full BRICS members even as most also hold Major Non-NATO Ally status with Washington or signed onto Trump's Board of Peace.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: MENA states are structurally hedged across both blocs' institutions, meaning forcing an exclusive alignment choice on them is likely to fail and alienate whichever power tries it.
insights.locator: The most telling arena for diplomatic hedging is participation in multilateral organizations.

insights.claim: MENA states are increasingly hedging via third parties beyond the U.S.-China binary: Saudi Arabia signed a 2025 mutual defense pact with Pakistan treating aggression against either as aggression against both, and Saudi Arabia and the UAE signed defense-cooperation memoranda with South Korea in February 2026.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: A third-party diversification trend (Pakistan, South Korea) that dilutes both US and Chinese leverage over Gulf security choices — watch for further security-guarantor diversification deals.
insights.locator: Saudi Arabia signed a strategic mutual defense agreement with Pakistan in 2025

insights.claim: The author's central thesis is that the U.S. and China have distinct, complementary strengths in MENA — U.S. military/security superiority versus China's commercial/economic rise and more favorable public perception — and that both powers benefit more from a tacit division of labor than from trying to squeeze each other out.
insights.speaker: Amr Hamzawy
insights.so_what: The bottom-line investment implication: MENA is not shaping up as a winner-take-all US-China contest, so capital allocated to the region should expect both powers' footprints (US security/AI infrastructure, Chinese trade/BRI) to persist and deepen in parallel rather than one displacing the other.
insights.quote: While Washington pursues hegemony at any cost, Beijing pursues strategic relations at the lowest cost possible.
insights.locator: More than anything, however, China and the United States have different visions
